content.problemStatement:
Chef has a garden of size N√óNN \times NN√óN. The garden is divided into N2N^2N2 squares of size 1√ó11\times 11√ó1 each.

Chef has plants in some unit squares of the garden, because of which, that part looks green.

Formally, for the ithi^{th}ith row (0‚â§i<N)(0 \le i \lt N)(0‚â§i<N), Chef has plants in all the columns from AiA_iAi‚Äã to BiB_iBi‚Äã  (both inclusive) where 0‚â§Ai‚â§Bi<N0 \le A_i \le B_i \lt N0‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§Bi‚Äã<N.

Help Chef find the **length** of the side of the **largest square** that is green.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer NNN, the size of the garden.
The next NNN lines contain two space-separated integers AiA_iAi‚Äã and BiB_iBi‚Äã, representing that plants are present in all the columns from AiA_iAi‚Äã to BiB_iBi‚Äã (both inclusive) of the ithi^{th}ith row.


Output Format
Output a single integer denoting the **length** of the side of the **largest square** that is green. In other words, output the **length** of the side of the square with **maximum** size, inside which **all** unit squares have plants.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Sample Explanation 111: The garden looks like:

The largest green square has vertices (1,1),(1,2),(2,2),(2,1)(1,1), (1,2), (2,2), (2,1)(1,1),(1,2),(2,2),(2,1). Thus, the length of the side of the largest green square is 222.
Sample Explanation 222: The garden looks like:

One of the largest green squares has vertices (0,2),(0,4),(2,4),(2,2)(0,2), (0,4), (2, 4), (2, 2)(0,2),(0,4),(2,4),(2,2). Thus, the length of the side of the largest green square is 333.
There may be other squares with same size but it can be proven that no square with length more than 333 is green.
